Are These Nursery Rhymes Actually About Death?

Origins, folklore, and the dark history beneath children's verses

Folklorists caution that many grim explanations emerged far more recently than the rhymes themselves — but some darker origins may contain at least a grain of historical truth.

Read the full article at History Facts ↗

Ring around the rosie
A pocket full of posies
Ashes, ashes
We all fall down
I of VI

"Ring Around the Rosie"

Likely a myth

The popular belief that this rhyme encodes the bubonic plague — the ring a rash, the posies herbs against disease, and "fall down" mass death — is almost universally rejected by folklorists. The rhyme wasn't recorded in print until the late 19th century, centuries after the Black Death and London's Great Plague. Scholars find no link to disease before the mid-20th century. Versions vary wildly, and in many the children simply pop back up — a poor metaphor for a pandemic. Most likely it was always a children's dancing game, nothing more.

London Bridge is falling down,
Falling down, falling down,
London Bridge is falling down,
My fair lady.
II of VI

"London Bridge Is Falling Down"

Possibly dark

Unlike most cases, this rhyme may carry a genuine echo of something ancient and disturbing. Early versions mention "set a man to watch all night" and reference an imprisoned figure — details that fascinate folklorists because legends of human sacrifice at bridges appear across Europe. The notion that a living person (often a child) was immured in a structure's foundations to appease river gods persists from Germany to Greece. Iona and Peter Opie called it "perhaps the only" nursery rhyme that genuinely preserves "the memory of a dark and terrible rite."

Humpty Dumpty sat on a wall,
Humpty Dumpty had a great fall.
All the king's horses and all the king's men
Couldn't put Humpty together again.
III of VI

"Humpty Dumpty"

Likely a myth

Claims that Humpty Dumpty was a royalist cannon toppled during the Siege of Colchester (1648) — or a siege engine at Gloucester — are dismissed by historians for lack of evidence. The simpler, older explanation is far more compelling: this was originally a riddle, and the answer was an egg. Early sources say so explicitly. Lewis Carroll's anthropomorphized egg in Through the Looking-Glass (1871) obscured this for generations. The strange details make sense once you know: riddles never give away their answer — and a broken egg, of course, cannot be reassembled by anyone.

Who killed Cock Robin?
"I," said the Sparrow,
"With my bow and arrow,
I killed Cock Robin."
IV of VI

"Who Killed Cock Robin?"

Debated

Uniquely, this rhyme wears its darkness openly — it is a miniature murder mystery with blood, burial, and grieving birds, first printed in the mid-18th century. Some scholars connect "Cock Robin" to the political downfall of British statesman Robert Walpole; others suggest the rhyme may preserve fragments of Norse mythology surrounding the death of the god Balder. Neither theory rests on solid evidence. More likely it belongs to a Victorian tradition of mock funerals and animal ballads — but the darker interpretations are at least intriguing enough that folklorists continue to debate them.

Jack and Jill went up the hill
To fetch a pail of water.
Jack fell down and broke his crown,
And Jill came tumbling after.
V of VI

"Jack and Jill"

Debated

The village of Kilmersdon in Somerset claims the rhyme for its own: Jack and Jill were real people, a quarry rock killed Jack, and Jill died in childbirth shortly after. A plaque and "Jack and Jill Hill" mark the spot to this day. Other theories tie the rhyme to Norse mythology (Hjúki and Bil, moon figures carrying water), or even the French Revolution — though the rhyme predates the guillotine by decades. A more prosaic and historically plausible reading links the names to 17th-century English measures of liquid: when the tax-reduced "jack" shrank, the "gill" came tumbling after.

Mary, Mary, quite contrary,
How does your garden grow?
With silver bells and cockle shells
And pretty maids all in a row.
VI of VI

"Mary, Mary, Quite Contrary"

Likely a myth

This rhyme has drawn some of the grimmest interpretations of all. One popular reading casts it as a portrait of Queen Mary I ("Bloody Mary"): the garden is England, and the silver bells and cockle shells are instruments of torture used in her persecution of Protestants. Others link it to Mary, Queen of Scots — the "pretty maids all in a row" supposedly her famous ladies-in-waiting. Folklorists, however, remain unconvinced by both. As the Opies observed, the various theories amount to "happy guessers" forcing hidden meanings onto a rhyme not recorded until the 18th century.

Adapted from "Are These 6 Famous Nursery Rhymes Actually About Death?" — History Facts, June 2026
